title: From SAP Monitoring to Observability at Scale
description: Bridges SAP's monitoring tools (ST05, transaction codes, CCMS, SAP Solution Manager) to FAANG's observability stack. Covers the three pillars (metrics, logs, traces), Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, distributed tracing, alerting philosophy, and the cultural shift from 'checking the system' to 'the system tells you when it's broken.'

sections.title: Monitoring vs Observability — The Mindset Shift

description.value:
In SAP, monitoring means: checking predefined dashboards and alerts (ST05 for SQL traces, ST22 for dump analysis, CCMS for system health, Solution Manager for alerting). You know what to check because you know what can go wrong. In FAANG, observability means: instrumenting your system so you can answer any question about its behavior without knowing the question in advance. This is the fundamental mindset shift.

Monitoring (SAP): 'I know what I need to check. I check it via transaction codes and dashboards.' Monitoring answers known unknowns. You know what could go wrong (table full, dump, RFC failure), so you monitor for it. The limitation: you can only monitor for things you expect. Unknown unknowns — the problems you didn't anticipate — are invisible until they cause a user-facing incident.

Observability (FAANG): 'I instrument my code to emit structured data. I explore the data to understand system behavior.' Observability answers unknown unknowns. You don't need to know what will go wrong in advance — you just need enough data to debug any problem when it occurs. Three pillars: metrics (what is happening?), logs (what happened in this specific case?), traces (how did this request flow through the system?).

Bridge concepts: (1) SAP ST05 trace = distributed trace (for a single system). Same concept: trace the path of a request through system components. ST05 shows SQL statements, RFC calls, table accesses. OpenTelemetry traces show service calls, database calls, queue operations. (2) SAP ST22 dump analysis = error logs with stack traces. Same concept: when something fails, capture the state at the point of failure. (3) SAP CCMS alerts = Prometheus alerts with Alertmanager. Same concept: define thresholds, get notified when breached. (4) SAP Solution Manager = Grafana dashboard. Same concept: visualize system health and performance.

sections.title: Metrics — From CCMS to Prometheus

description.value:
SAP CCMS (Computing Center Management System) monitors system metrics: CPU usage, memory, disk space, dialog response time, update processing, RFC queue depth. Prometheus scrapes the same types of metrics from FAANG services: request latency, error rate, throughput, resource utilization, queue depth. The concepts are identical. The implementation is different.

Key metric types (both worlds): (1) Counters: Cumulative values that only increase (total requests, total errors, total bytes sent). Prometheus counter, SAP: cumulative stats in ST03N. (2) Gauges: Values that go up and down (current CPU, current memory, active connections). Prometheus gauge, SAP: live monitoring in CCMS. (3) Histograms: Distribution of values over time (request latency buckets). Prometheus histogram, SAP: response time distribution in ST03N. (4) Summaries: Quantile-based distributions (P50, P95, P99 latency). Prometheus summary, SAP: percentile analysis in Solution Manager.

The RED and USE methods: (1) RED (Rate, Errors, Duration): For each service, track: request rate (R), error rate (E), request duration (D). This is the minimum set of metrics every service should expose. SAP equivalent: RFC call statistics (rate), application errors (error rate), dialog response time (duration). (2) USE (Utilization, Saturation, Errors): For each resource (CPU, memory, disk, network), track: utilization (percentage busy), saturation (queue depth), errors (failure count). SAP equivalent: CCMS resource monitoring. SAP engineers who understand RED and USE can design monitoring for any system.

sections.codeBlock:
# Prometheus Metric Definitions (with SAP Analogies)

// RED metrics for a payment service
# HELP payment_requests_total Total number of payment requests
# TYPE payment_requests_total counter
payment_requests_total{status="success"} 102400
payment_requests_total{status="failed"} 340

# HELP payment_request_duration_seconds Request latency in seconds
# TYPE payment_request_duration_seconds histogram
payment_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.1"} 85000
payment_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.5"} 98000
payment_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="1.0"} 101000
payment_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="+Inf"} 102740
payment_request_duration_seconds_sum 18432
payment_request_duration_seconds_count 102740

// SAP analogy: ST03N shows response time distribution for each transaction
// CCMS shows current CPU, memory, and dialog step utilization
// The RED method translates directly: Rate = ST03N calls/hour,
// Errors = ST22 dump count, Duration = ST03N response time

sections.title: Logging — From SAP Application Logs to Structured Logging

description.value:
SAP application logs (transaction SLG1) store structured logs with object, sub-object, and message fields. This is closer to FAANG's structured logging than traditional syslog-based logging. SAP engineers who understand the value of structured logs have a head start in FAANG's logging culture.

SAP application logs: Logged with: Object (ZPAYMENT), Sub-object (PROCESSING), Message type (S/E/W/I), Message text, User, Date, Time, Transaction code. The combination of object and sub-object provides a structured way to filter and analyze logs. SAP's logging is implicitly structured — each log entry has typed fields.

FAANG structured logging: Each log line is a JSON object with typed fields. Example: {"timestamp":"2026-06-25T10:00:00Z", "level":"error", "service":"payment", "trace_id":"abc123", "message":"Payment processing failed", "error":"INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS", "user_id":"user_456"}. This is the same concept as SAP application logs but: (a) fields are defined by the developer and can be anything, (b) logs are aggregated across services (SAP logs are per-system), (c) logs are searchable with full-text and field-level queries.

The logging mindset shift: In SAP, logs are checked when something goes wrong (transaction SLG1, ST22). In FAANG, logs are written assuming someone will need to analyze them — every request should produce a traceable log trail. Bridge: 'I used SAP's structured application logging (SLG1) with categorized messages. In a FAANG context, I would apply the same structured logging principles using JSON-formatted logs with consistent fields across services, enabling centralized log aggregation and analysis.'

sections.title: Distributed Tracing — From ST05 to OpenTelemetry

description.value:
SAP ST05 (SQL Trace) shows the database operations triggered by a single user interaction. It traces: SQL statements, table operations, RFC calls, and authorization checks within a single SAP system. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing shows the path of a single request through multiple services, databases, and queues. The concept is the same — trace one request through the system — but the scope is different (single system vs distributed system).

Distributed tracing concepts: (1) Trace: The end-to-end path of a single request through the system. Represented by a trace ID that propagates across service boundaries. SAP equivalent: ST05 trace for a single user interaction (but limited to one system). (2) Span: A single unit of work within a trace — one service call, one database query, one queue operation. Each span has a start time, duration, status, and optional tags. SAP equivalent: one line in ST05 trace showing a SQL statement with duration. (3) Context propagation: The trace ID is passed from service to service via HTTP headers (traceparent) or message metadata. SAP equivalent: no equivalent across systems — ST05 traces are per-system. (4) Sampling: Not every request is traced (that would be too expensive). A sampling strategy determines which requests to trace (e.g., trace 1 in 100 requests, or trace all requests with errors). SAP equivalent: ST05 is explicitly started and stopped by the developer.

Why this matters for SAP engineers: Distributed tracing is the single most important debugging tool in a microservices architecture. When a payment fails, the trace shows exactly which service returned an error, how long each call took, and what the error was. SAP engineers who understand this concept can transfer their debugging skills from ST05 to distributed systems. Bridge: 'I used SAP ST05 to trace the database operations of individual user requests. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing applies the same concept at a larger scale — tracing requests across microservices with the same goal of understanding request flow and identifying bottlenecks.'

sections.title: Alerting Philosophy — From 'Check the System' to 'Page the Engineer'

description.value:
SAP alerting is reactive: alerts fire when predefined thresholds are breached (CPU > 90%, disk full, dump occurred). An admin checks the alert, investigates, and resolves. FAANG alerting is proactive: alerts fire when user-facing impact is detected or imminent. The goal is to alert on symptoms (high error rate, high latency) rather than causes (high CPU, full disk).

SAP alerting characteristics: (1) Alert on resource metrics (CPU, memory, disk). (2) Thresholds are static (same for all systems, all times of day). (3) Alerts go to a monitoring team, not the development team. (4) Response time: hours to days (admins triage during business hours). (5) False positive rate: high (static thresholds don't account for normal variation).

FAANG alerting characteristics: (1) Alert on service-level objectives (error rate > 0.1%, latency P99 > 500ms). (2) Thresholds are dynamic (based on historical baselines, time-of-day patterns). (3) Alerts go to the on-call engineer (the person who wrote the code). (4) Response time: minutes (on-call engineer responds within 15 minutes of page, 24/7). (5) False positive rate: low (alerts are tuned to minimize noise).

The alerting philosophy shift: In SAP, alerts are information ('something might be wrong, check it when you have time'). In FAANG, alerts are urgent ('something is wrong right now, drop everything and fix it'). Every alert should be: (a) actionable (someone must do something), (b) urgent (needs attention within minutes), (c) specific (which service, which metric, which threshold). If an alert does not meet all three criteria, it should be a dashboard, not a page. Bridge: 'I understand that alerting should be based on user-facing symptoms, not system-internal metrics. I would apply the SLO-based alerting approach: define the desired user experience (99.9% success rate, P99 latency < 500ms), measure it with RED metrics, and alert when the SLO is at risk of being breached.'

decisionTree.prompt: Should this condition be an alert, a dashboard, or a log?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: Does the condition require immediate human action?

branches.condition: Yes — user-facing impact is happening or imminent, and an engineer must act within minutes
branches.path: Create an alert that pages the on-call engineer via PagerDuty/OpsGenie. Include: the specific metric breaching, the current value vs threshold, the affected service, and a runbook link. Set the severity based on user impact: P0 = all users affected, P1 = subset of users affected, P2 = no user impact but risk of future impact.

branches.condition: No — useful information but does not require immediate action or engineer knows about it already
branches.path: Create a dashboard panel or Grafana annotation. This is a 'watch item' that the team reviews during regular on-call handoff or weekly review. Examples: slowly filling disk, gradually increasing latency, batch job that took longer than usual but still within SLA.

branches.condition: Not sure — could be an alert but you want to observe the pattern first
branches.path: Create a dashboard first. Monitor for 2-4 weeks. If the condition never triggers during the observation period, delete the dashboard. If it triggers occasionally but doesn't require action, keep it as a dashboard. If it triggers and an engineer wishes they had been paged, promote it to an alert. This 'dashboard-first, alert-later' approach minimizes alert fatigue.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The most common observability mistake is alerting on everything. Each alert that pages an engineer costs that engineer 15-30 minutes of context-switching time (waking up, logging in, assessing the situation). A high false-positive rate leads to 'alert fatigue' — engineers ignore pages, including genuine ones. Start with no alerts. Add alerts one at a time, each with a specific, actionable, urgent condition. Fewer, better-calibrated alerts are more effective than many noisy ones.

details: Staff+ Insight: SAP engineers often have strong monitoring experience but frame it in SAP-specific terms. The framing matters: 'I used ST05 to trace SQL queries and ST22 to analyze dumps' makes you sound like an SAP admin. 'I designed a monitoring strategy using transaction-level traces and error analysis, enabling the team to identify and resolve performance bottlenecks within minutes' sounds like an observability engineer. The technology is different but the principles are identical: define what matters (metrics), capture what happened (logs), trace the request (traces), and alert on user impact. SAP engineers who can articulate these principles without SAP jargon will be seen as strong candidates for FAANG observability roles.
